title: Buontalenti Grotto - Origins of Liberal Education
highlights: Buontalenti, Bernardo (Arezzo 1511- Florence 1574) (Florence, 1531-Florence, 1608)  ...    In 1513, Francesco I, Bernardo Buontalenti’s former student, hired the artist to design a setting for four unfinished marble sculptures by Michelangelo, known as the “Bound Slaves.” The prince wanted a unique display for the sculptures located in the Boboli Gardens in Florence, Italy. Buontalenti suggested that a rustic grotto of chambers encrusted with  ...    tigers, monkeys, and birds (“Buontalenti, Bernardo”). Michelangelo’s four Slaves were placed in the corners of this interior space. Because this masterpiece was created during the Renaissance, the artists were consequently liberally educated. In relation to Michelangelo, he came from a higher social stratum, thus he received a liberal education. ### The Grotta Grande | 1583-93 Rock Boboli Gardens, Florence //Welcome to the Web Gallery of Art! Please enter here to visit the  ...    | The Large Grotto (two smaller ones are located behind it) was completed under Buontalenti's supervision between 1583 and 1593. The four corners housed Michelangelo's unfinished sculptures of the four Slaves intended for the tomb of Pope Julius II in Rome, which had been acquired by Cosimo I from the sculptor's heirs. title: Le sculture della Grotta del Buontalenti
highlights:
Fu Francesco I nel 1583 a commissionare a Bernardo Buontalenti una grande grotta, un luogo che sarebbe diventato il più suggestivo di tutto il Giardino di Boboli. Ci vollero dieci anni per portare a termine l’opera ed è un vero peccato che da secoli non sia più possibile ammirare i  ...    Varcando la soglia della Grotta del Buontalenti ci si trova nella prima camera e non si può fare a meno di notare le copie dei miei quattro prigioni. Gli originali erano stati donati a Cosimo I de’ Medici da mio nipote Lionardo che volle subito farle inserire in questo nuovo contesto. title: Buontalenti's Grotto in Florence: Visit and Practical Information
highlights: work is an artificial grotto whose construction was started by Giorgio Vasari and later completed by Bernardo Buontalenti on behalf of Francesco I de’ Medici. It represents one of the most important examples of Mannerism as well as an architecturally extraordinary work. He skilfully blends painting, architecture and sculpture for a completely unique and original result. It was also mentioned by the writer Dan Brown in his famous novel “Inferno”, set almost entirely in Florence.  ...    to merge to give life to zoomorphic and anthropomorphic figures.
